topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox, a stark white rectangle against the muted gray of my desktop. I clicked it open, the subject line—"Scholarship Application Update"—offering no surprise. I skimmed the boilerplate text, the polite rejection phrasing washing over me like lukewarm water. My fingers hovered over the 'delete' button, then settled on simply closing the window. There was a slight, almost imperceptible slump in my shoulders as I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning softly beneath me. The weight of the application, the hours poured into essays and recommendations, felt suddenly insignificant, like a discarded wrapper. I didn't move to reread anything; the information was processed, filed away, and then dismissed. I stood up, stretched my arms above my head with a slow, deliberate motion, and walked to the window, watching the indifferent traffic flow below.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the text blurred into meaningless shapes. A faint pressure built behind my eyes, a dull ache that mirrored the flatness of my mood. I didn't feel the sting of disappointment; instead, there was just a quiet acknowledgment that the outcome was what it was. I picked up a pen, twirled it between my fingers, and watched the ink dry on the desk blotter. The rejection letter sat there, a piece of paper with a definitive, final stamp. I didn't bother to fold it neatly. Instead, I tossed it toward the recycling bin with a casual flick of my wrist. My breathing remained even, slow and shallow, as I started tidying the surrounding papers, stacking them methodically. It was just another administrative hurdle cleared, nothing more, nothing less.

[story 3]

"Denied." The single word echoed in the silence of my small room. I let the sound hang there, unattached to any strong feeling. I didn't check my phone for updates or reread the accompanying notes. My gaze drifted toward the windowpane, where the afternoon sun cast long, unremarkable shadows across the floor. A slight twitch played at the corner of my mouth, a gesture devoid of humor or sadness. I walked over to my bookshelf, running a hand over the spines of unrelated novels. The scholarship represented a path, and now that path had simply closed. I picked up a textbook, opened it to a random page, and began reading, the words swimming by without any real engagement. It was just a shift in variables, an event that required no significant emotional response.
